# AI Features: CKEditor vs. Competitors

**Last updated:** April 2026 (Froala updated to reflect 5.1 AI Assist plugin; TinyMCE updated to reflect current AI Chat, Review, Actions, and permissions)
**Scope:** AI capabilities only — writing assistance, LLM integration, customization, and deployment
**Editors covered:** CKEditor, TinyMCE, Tiptap, Froala, Quill, ProseMirror, Lexical

---

## How to Use This Document

Use this table in sales and product conversations when a prospect asks how CKEditor's AI compares to the competition. It focuses exclusively on AI — for a full feature comparison including collaboration and compliance, see [competitive-analysis.md](../../research/competitive-analysis.md).

---

## AI Feature Comparison

| Feature | CKEditor | TinyMCE | Tiptap | Froala | Quill | ProseMirror | Lexical |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Native AI writing assistance** | ✅ Deep integration | ✅ Chat, Review, Actions — deeply featured | ✅ Platform tier | ✅ AI Assist plugin (5.1, 2026) | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| **AI Chat interface** | ✅ Full chat with context, reasoning, web search | ✅ Full AI Chat with history, file/URL context, web search, reasoning mode | ✅ Conversational AI included | ✅ In-editor popup with chat and text actions | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| **Inline AI commands / Quick Actions** | ✅ On selected text — rewrite, simplify, expand, fix grammar | ✅ Built-in + fully customizable Quick Actions with custom prompts | ✅ Slash command-based | ✅ Select text → choose action → instant improvement | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| **AI Review (on-demand quality check)** | ✅ Grammar, tone, style, clarity — inline suggestions | ✅ 5 built-in types (Proofread, Clarity, Readability, Length, Tone) + custom reviews via API | ❌ Not confirmed | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| **AI Agent (autonomous document editing)** | ⚠️ Partial via MCP (On-Premises, March 2026) | ❌ | ✅ Reads, plans, edits documents — launched May 2025 | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| **Multi-LLM provider support** | ✅ OpenAI, Anthropic, Google Gemini | ✅ OpenAI (GPT-5 family), Anthropic (Claude 4.6/4.5), Google Gemini + Auto mode | ✅ Configurable | ✅ OpenAI, self-hosted models, custom backends | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| **Bring your own LLM / API key** | ✅ On-Premises (March 2026) | ⚠️ Managed API only — no BYOLLM; on-prem with custom models in early access (mid-2026) | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes — direct browser connection or via custom backend | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| **Custom prompts & commands** | ✅ Custom Quick Actions and Reviews | ✅ Custom Quick Actions via config; custom Reviews via API | ✅ AI Toolkit (beta, Sept 2025) | ⚠️ Custom tone presets (Professional, Casual, Enthusiastic); custom request handler | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| **AI UI customization** | ✅ Sidebar or overlay, left/right placement, full configurability | ⚠️ Configurable menus and actions; UI framework is Tiny's own — limited structural override | ✅ Headless — full UI control | ⚠️ Popup UI included; programmatic API to trigger AI from anywhere in your app | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| **Context injection (files, URLs, text)** | ✅ Inject documents, URLs, or reference content into chat | ✅ File uploads (PDF, DOCX, images, MD, HTML, text), URLs, custom external data sources | ⚠️ Not prominently featured | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| **AI Chat history (per document)** | ✅ Persistent, renameable, resumable per document | ✅ Persistent, filterable, organized by date; 12-month retention | ❌ Not confirmed | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| **Model selection per user** | ✅ Users choose model; integrators define available options | ✅ User model switching configurable; availability controlled per subscription tier via JWT | ⚠️ Partial | ⚠️ Custom metadata (user ID, model settings) passed per request | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| **AI Profiles (tone, style, vocabulary presets)** | ❌ Not yet available | ❌ | ❌ | ⚠️ Basic tone options only | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| **Web search in AI Chat** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes — real-time web search + reasoning mode in Chat | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| **MCP integration** | ✅ On-Premises (March 2026) — connect tools, data sources, agents | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| **AI response interception / content control** | ✅ Full control via custom endpoints and permissions | ✅ Accept/reject individual suggestions with diff view and preview; bulk apply/skip | ⚠️ Partial | ✅ Intercept, validate, filter, or reject AI responses before insertion | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| **Rich text format preservation** | ✅ AI works with full HTML — preserves all editor formatting | ⚠️ Not explicitly confirmed at full HTML level | ⚠️ Markdown-only in AI Agent | ⚠️ Not confirmed — extent of HTML preservation unclear | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| **RBAC for AI features** | ✅ Per-user, per-tier, per-feature access control | ✅ JWT-based hierarchical permissions — per-user, per-model, per-provider, per-feature | ⚠️ Partial | ⚠️ User consent flag required (aiSupplementalTermsAccepted); deeper RBAC not confirmed | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| **On-premises AI deployment** | ✅ Available (March 2026) | ⚠️ Early access — on-prem AI roadmap for mid-2026; editor self-hostable but AI is cloud-only today | ✅ Self-hostable (paid tier) | ✅ On-premises license available; self-hosted models keep data on your servers | ✅ (but no AI features) | ✅ (but no AI features) | ✅ (but no AI features) |
| **AI usage monitoring & analytics** | ✅ Insights Panel in Customer Portal | ❌ Not confirmed | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| **Deep integration with editor features** | ✅ Works with 300+ CKEditor features natively | ⚠️ Plugin layer — integration depth with all core editor features not fully confirmed | ⚠️ Limited — markdown-based AI layer | ⚠️ Plugin-based — integration depth with core editor features not confirmed | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |

---

## AI Readiness Summary

| Editor | AI Depth | LLM Flexibility | Developer Control | Deployment Options | Overall |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CKEditor** | Full: chat, review, quick actions, profiles, context injection | OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini + BYOLLM (On-Prem) | Full UI and prompt configurability, RBAC, MCP | SaaS + On-Premises | **Strong** |
| **TinyMCE** | AI Chat (history, file/URL context, web search, reasoning), AI Review (5 types + custom), customizable Quick Actions | OpenAI (GPT-5), Anthropic (Claude 4.6/4.5), Gemini; managed API — no BYOLLM today | JWT-based RBAC; custom prompts/actions via config; programmatic REST API; limited UI structural override | SaaS; on-prem AI in early access (mid-2026) | **Strong (with caveats)** |
| **Tiptap** | Generation, AI Agent, custom building blocks | Configurable + BYOLLM | High (headless) — build your own AI UX | SaaS + Self-hosted (paid) | **Strong (build-it-yourself)** |
| **Froala** | AI Assist (5.1): in-editor chat popup, inline commands, tone adjustment, translation | OpenAI, self-hosted models, custom backends | Moderate — custom request handler, response interception, programmatic API | On-premises license available with self-hosted model support | **Moderate (growing)** |
| **Quill** | None | None | None | Open source (no AI) | **None** |
| **ProseMirror** | None — AI left to the application developer | None built-in | Total (build everything yourself) | Open source (no AI) | **None** |
| **Lexical** | None — AI left to the application developer | None built-in | Total (build everything yourself) | Open source (no AI) | **None** |

---

## Key Differentiators: Where CKEditor AI Stands Apart

**1. Rich text format preservation**
CKEditor AI is the only solution confirmed to fully understand and preserve HTML formatting during AI edits. Tiptap's AI Agent works with simplified markdown. TinyMCE's HTML preservation at the AI layer is not explicitly documented. CKEditor AI works natively with the same document model the editor uses — all 300+ features stay intact after every AI operation.

**2. MCP integration**
As of April 2026, CKEditor is the only editor in this market with native MCP support. This enables connecting external tools, data sources, and AI agents to the editor's backend — accessible via AI Chat or programmatically. No competitor has shipped this.

**3. On-premises AI with custom LLM support — available now**
CKEditor's On-Premises distribution supports custom LLMs and MCP-connected models, available since March 2026. TinyMCE's on-premises AI is in early access with a mid-2026 roadmap. Froala supports self-hosted models but without the governance layer. CKEditor is the only editor where on-prem AI with BYOLLM is production-ready today.

**4. AI usage monitoring & analytics**
The Customer Portal Insights Panel gives integrators visibility into per-operation AI usage and credit consumption. TinyMCE and Tiptap have no confirmed equivalent. This matters for enterprise deployments managing cost governance across large user bases.

**6. Complete out-of-the-box product vs. assembly required**
TinyMCE now has strong AI depth, but the UI framework is Tiny's own with limited structural customization. Tiptap's AI Toolkit gives developers low-level building blocks. CKEditor ships a complete, configurable AI product — Chat, Review, Quick Actions, Profiles — that works with all 300+ editor features without additional assembly.

---

## Notes on Competitors Without AI

**Quill, ProseMirror, and Lexical** have no AI features and no published AI roadmap. Any AI capability on these platforms requires the application developer to build the full integration layer — LLM orchestration, prompt management, streaming, UI, formatting preservation, and context handling. This is typically a 3–6 month engineering project before reaching feature parity with CKEditor AI's current baseline.

---

*Review this document quarterly. TinyMCE's AI has expanded significantly — validate their on-prem AI status and BYOLLM availability before sales calls, as both are expected mid-2026. Tiptap's AI roadmap also moves quickly — confirm AI Agent and AI Toolkit status. Froala's AI story changed with 5.1; re-validate depth of HTML preservation and RBAC before using in a competitive conversation.*

Basics



CKEditor AI
Tailor AI behavior to your application, content, and workflow

Bring AI directly into your editor with fully integrated UI and features that help users brainstorm, rewrite, polish and revise content without context switching.
Empower teams with prebuilt and editable AI commands that match your use case.
Store and reuse complex prompts for structured, repeatable tasks. Create tone, structure, and vocabulary presets for consistent output.

Note for PMM DRI: This slide will become your customer-facing slide to promote the feature - AEs should be able to easily add this to customer-facing presentation. 

CKEditor AI: Executive Summary

By integrating AI writing assistance directly into the editing experience, CKEditor AI empowers authors with real-time AI writing support, streamlines content creation, and enhances editorial workflows across a wide range of use cases – from productivity boosts and proof-reading to content quality and consistency.
For developers
Seamless integration of AI features via plugin architecture
Fine-grained control over AI interactions within the editor, enabling contextual prompts and UI customization
Works with various LLM providers abstracted via a common interface
Supports MCP: connect external tools, data sources, and AI agents on the backend integration side — accessible via AI Chat or programmatically
Custom LLMs supported via MCP in On-Premises distribution — use your own models without vendor lock-in
Extensible with server-side endpoints and custom prompt templates to meet domain-specific needs
Reduces frontend development burden through prebuilt, configurable AI UX components
For end users
On-demand suggestions, rewrites, and grammar fixes embedded directly in the editor UI
Maintains writing flow by providing instant feedback and actionable content improvements
Improves confidence and consistency for non-native writers or domain experts lacking editorial expertise
Adaptive to various content types – from legal docs and marketing copy to code documentation
Keeping rich text formatting intact, reducing the hours spent on formatting content pasted from external AI tools

Solution & Customer Context



Customer Feedback

Feedback from current customers 

Customer Pain Points to Address

Seamless, Scalable AI-Powered EditingPain Point: Implementing AI features requires costly orchestration of LLMs, different tools, prompt handling, UI integration and rich text formatting preservation.
Solution: CKEditor AI offers a fully integrated, plug-and-play AI writing assistant that embeds into your tech stack with minimal configuration. You gain advanced GenAI capabilities without building infrastructure from scratch 
Unified Content Creation WorkflowPain Point: Writers switch between standalone AI tools and the editor, leading to workflow fragmentation and copy-paste inefficiencies.
Solution: Native AI chat, drafting, and editing within CKEditor eliminate context switching and unify research, creation, and formatting in a single environment.




Customer Pain Points to Address

Fast, Repeatable Edits with AIPain Point: Routine tasks like rewording or summarizing require repetitive prompts in chat interfaces.
Solution: AI Quick Actions allow users to instantly apply common transformations - like simplify, expand, or fix grammar - directly on selected content in the editor, speeding up everyday work.
High-Quality, Context-Aware AI OutputPain Point: Generic AI tools often hallucinate or miss the mark due to lack of domain or document context.
Solution: CKEditor AI allows users to inject documents, URLs, or reference files into conversations to ground the model, delivering more accurate, relevant responses.
Reliable Document Quality ControlPain Point: Manual proofreading is slow and inconsistent, especially across large or regulated content.
Solution: AI Review surfaces grammar, tone, clarity, and style issues directly in the editor with inline suggestions for review, helping teams enforce brand or legal standards.


Customer Pain Points to Address

Reusable Institutional KnowledgePain Point: Once a user closes an AI session, all context and insights are lost, leading to repeated effort.
Solution: AI Chat History preserves conversations per document, allowing teams to revisit, rename, or resume AI threads-turning chats into ongoing, collaborative assets.
Optimized AI for Different Use CasesPain Point: One-size-fits-all models don’t suit every task; power users want control while integrators need cost management.
Solution: Model Selection enables users to choose the best-fit LLM for their task while integrators define which models are available per plan or product tier.
Personalized, Efficient AI InteractionsPain Point: Rewriting prompts and adjusting tone or structure manually reduces productivity.
Solution: Custom Commands let users define tone, structure, and reuse task-specific prompts, saving time and increasing output consistency.


Customer Pain Points to Address

Enterprise-Grade AI Governance and UI Consistency
Pain Point: Inconsistent AI implementation across features increases technical debt and UX friction.
Solution: CKEditor’s shared AI infrastructure ensures robust telemetry, error handling, moderation, and streaming output across all AI features - ready for enterprise deployment


Personas

Buyer Persona
[Roles]: Product Manager, Product Owner, Software Architect, CTO, Engineering Manager, Chief AI Officer (CAIO),  AI Lead, AI Compliance Lead, AI Governance Officer
[Industries]: SaaS Platforms, CRM, Customer Support Platforms, Internal Tools (e.g., HR or Finance Portals), Content Management Systems (CMS), Knowledge Bases, Compliance-Heavy Platforms (e.g., Legal, Healthcare, Finance), Content Collaboration platforms, 

Engineering manager narrative
Before
Lukas, an engineering manager at a SaaS company, was under pressure to integrate AI writing assistance into their web-based document editor. His product team needed features like summarization, tone adjustment, and grammar suggestions to stay competitive - but building them in-house meant orchestrating LLM APIs, prompt engineering, chat UI, session storage, and permissions logic.
He scoped the effort and quickly realized the complexity: it would take months, multiple engineers, and constant upkeep just to match the baseline functionality of tools like ChatGPT. Even worse, it risked fragmenting the editor experience they’d spent years perfecting. Lukas needed a way to integrate GenAI without rebuilding their stack or compromising UX.



Engineering manager narrative
After
By adopting CKEditor AI, Lukas’ team plugged enterprise-ready AI functionality directly into their existing editor. No orchestration layer, no custom UI scaffolding. Just drop-in AI Chat, AI Quick Actions for inline edits, AI Review, and contextual-grounded responses - all deeply integrated with their document model and permissions system.
Instead of writing AI infrastructure, Lukas’ team focused on product value - tuning AI commands for their domain, enabling model selection per pricing tier, and customizing user access via configuration.
What once looked like a six-month engineering project became a two-week integration. Now they deliver a polished, scalable AI writing experience - without the technical debt or maintenance burden.

Technical product manager narrative

BeforeAnne manages a product team building a document workflow tool for compliance-heavy industries. While their CKEditor-based editor provided rich formatting and export capabilities, content teams frequently relied on external GenAI tools for rewriting, tone adjustments, and basic QA.
Writers copied content into ChatGPT, reworded sections, then pasted results back into the editor - often introducing errors, inconsistencies, or compliance risks. The back-and-forth also slowed delivery, especially when editors had to rework tone or apply house style across dozens of documents. Anne was also concerned about data leaving their environment without proper governance.
Her team needed an integrated way to give writers AI support within the editor - without losing control, auditability, or productivity.

Technical product manager narrative

AfterWith CKEditor AI, Anne’s team embedded a full suite of AI features directly into their document editor. AI Quick Actions allowed users to instantly rewrite, summarize, or correct text in-place. For more nuanced edits, the AI Chat gave content creators contextual help - grounded in internal style guides and past documents via contextual file uploads. AI Review tools helped automate tone, grammar, and structure checks at scale.
Now, Anne’s team delivers faster, more accurate content workflows with full compliance visibility. No more external tools. No more copy-paste errors. Just streamlined, on-brand, AI-assisted writing - baked right into the editing experience.

AI not handling some complex tasks well due to current LLM limitations

No ability to use your own API keys, or custom models in V1 (coming in Q1 2026)

Excited About?


Ability to quickly implement AI writing features inside your app with drop-in components (including UI)
Tailoring AI to your content workflow and brand needs (proper context and voice)
Predefining AI actions
Fine-grained feature, context, and LLMs access control




FAQ from the clients

How much does it cost?
CKEditor AI is offered as an add-on to existing editor plans, using a simple and scalable subscription-plus-usage pricing model. Customers choose from three service tiers (Small, Medium, Large), each with a fixed monthly or annual fee that includes a credit allowance for AI-powered actions.

If customers exceed their monthly allowance, predictable overage fees apply. These units act as a virtual currency that hides the complexity of token-based pricing from AI vendors like OpenAI or Anthropic, allowing customers to track usage clearly without needing to understand token math. Advanced customers benefit from full visibility into usage, environment-specific feature controls, and trial dashboards that help determine the right plan. 

The result is a flexible, future-proof pricing structure that aligns 
with enterprise procurement needs and scales
 as AI adoption grows.

Here’s a detailed breakdown of pricing strategy.

FAQ from the clients

What is LLM?
LLM (Large Language Model) equals “AI Model” term that we often use internally. It’s a text engine trained on huge amounts of examples so it can understand what you write and respond in fluent, human-like language. Examples: Claude Sonnet, GPT 5, Gemini Pro 3

How to calculate AI tokens?
Let's start with what is a token in AI lingo - "A token is just a tiny chunk of text the AI counts and works with – like a word fragment. AI vendor pricing and limits are based on how many tokens (chunks of text) go into and come out of the AI."

A normal A4 page of english text (around 400–600 words) is roughly 500–800 tokens. However, there is a lot of variables that impact how many tokens LLM will consume.

In practice, you should not try to estimate specific token consumption as there is no reliable way to do that.
If you'd like to help our customers understand the costs of using CKEditor AI, it's best for them to use our Trial, perform typical operations that they expect to happen in their application and inspect usage in the Insights Panel within our Customer Portal.

Important note: tokens and token consumption are integral part of how LLMs operate - problem with estimating the consumption is not at all specific to our products and appears in any AI application.

FAQ from the clients

How can I know how much a specific CKEditor AI operation would cost in terms of credits?
We have a table that translates the cost of operations in credits per specific LLM: 

Is there on-premise distribution?
On-premise solution is planned for Q1 2026
Which LLM providers are available and which models can we use in CKEditor AI?
We start with models from 3 major providers: OpenAI, Anthropic and Gemini. Exact model list is not defined yet but our goal is always to at least provide a frontier + cost-efficient model from each provider. LLM market evolves rapidly so we have a built-in mechanism that allows us to introduce new models on the fly, as long as it's good enough to support CKEditor AI's features properly. We do not limit ourselves to only these providers and we might introduce support for others in the future.

Do we support RAG?
Not at the moment, but we might enable integrating own RAG implementations for customers through MCP as a part of On-Premises distribution. However, this is not 100% confirmed yet at the time of writingWhat is RAG (Retrieval-Augumented Generation)? 
By using RAG, before answering, the AI Model can search through large amounts of stored information (docs, knowledge base, etc.) and then respond based on it, instead of relying on its training data or specific context attached  manually by the user. 
The simplest example would be a case where user knows that the information they need is somewhere in their Notion, but doesn’t know where exactly. With RAG, AI could perform the search across the whole Notion and find required information on its own.


FAQ from the clients

Can I use my own API keys, custom models?
Not yet however we plan to allow using own API keys for models we support in  Q1 2026 as a part of on-premise.Ability to use “custom” models from outside the list of models that we officially support is still under research. Different custom models might have different requirements in terms of integration. MCP tooling support is now available in the On-Premises distribution (since March 2026), enabling custom AI models and tools to be connected as external services within client apps.

Note: By ‘custom model’, customers might mean fine-tuned models, which means they took an existing model and taught it something specific to their use-case.“Think of an old radio where you slowly turn the dial until the static clears and the station comes in. Or imagine a sound mixer with many knobs: you don’t rebuild the whole system, you adjust the balance so the music fits your taste.
Fine-tuning an AI model works in a similar way. You start with a base model that already “knows” a lot, and then you nudge it towards what matters for your context.”

Can I use my custom commands from AI Assistant?
Current AI Assistant is similar to Quick Actions in CKEditor AI and there will be a possibility to transfer the AI Assistant actions 1:1 into CKEditor AI.
Is my data used to train LLMs?
No, it’s not. 

FAQ from the clients

How can I monitor activity of my users and their usage?
Customers can use Insights Panel within out Customer Portal to investigate details of each operation in their app, including credits consumption of CKEditor AI.

Does CKEditor AI support MCP servers?
Yes. MCP support launched in March 2026 as part of the On-Premises distribution. You can connect your MCP server to CKEditor AI to pull fresh external knowledge into responses, prompt external tools directly from the editor, and integrate with data sources and AI agents in your infrastructure.
Learning resource: MCP - short intro for non-tech people
“MCP is just a protocol to define various tools and resources for the AI agent to do some specific tasks. It’s a streamlined framework that anyone can use to create a server and give access to the AI. It can increase the potential of doing real tasks with AI instead of just having conversation.”

Does CKEditor AI support AI agents?
“AI Agent” is a wide term that can be used to describe different things. Typically, by “AI agent” customers might mean autonomous AI model that can perform different tasks automatically across the system/application, used to automate repetitive workflows. At this moment, we don’t offer a way to integrate CKEditor AI into such workflows, but this is now partially enabled through MCP support, available since March 2026 in the On-Premises distribution.
Feedback needed: “What you’d expect such agent to do?/ What problem you’d like to solve by it?”

FAQ from the clients

To what extend I can customize AI features?
You can create your own prompts to define both custom Quick Actions and custom Reviews.
There is a possibility to add custom context sources to be available for end users (URLs to files / text content pulled from the database)
UI-wise, CKEditor AI can be displayed as a sidebar, or an overlay. There are also settings available to adjust UI for left/right hand side placement.
Where is my data stored and how is it processed?
Everything is stored in CKEditor Cloud Services and follows same rules and patterns as all other data we store for the editor features. For a full security breakdown go to the security section on our homepage. However, bear in mind that queries to LLMs together with all data required to perform the operation is being processed by the selected LLM provider.

Which models available in CKEditor AI are best for which use case?
We recommend using the default Agent/Auto mode which picks the best model for the job. In the future, we plan to expand its classification capabilities so generally it should be most reliable for general use.
Ability to choose specific models depends on end user’s preference mostly and is generally aimed towards “power users”. From integrator perspective, state-of-the-art models (GPT-5, Sonnet 4.5) are definitely going to perform better, but they are also more expensive. For simple scenarios, it might make more sense to limit choice to more cost-efficient models that will be good enough, but a lot cheaper (Claude Haiku 4.5, Gemini 2.5 Flash)

FAQ from the clients

Will AI features impact performance or editor size?
In general, CKEditor AI plugin shouldn’t have a significant impact on the performance and bundle size of CKEditor 5 on its own, so customers don’t need to be afraid of their apps slowing down suddenly.
However, AI operations themselves can be vulnerable to some performance issues in extreme scenarios (very large amount of content in the editor for example).

We were performing performance tests of CKEditor AI on 50+ pages long documents and we didn’t observe significant UX degradation, we also already have a few technical optimizations implemented to handle larger data quite well.

FAQ from the clients

Is there a way to implement Role-based Access Control (RBAC) for CKEditor AI?
Yes, you can regulate which users have access to which CKEditor AI features. You can control access to AI features, models, and capabilities based on user roles, subscription tiers, and organizational requirements.
More in the docs: https://ckeditor.com/docs/cs/latest/guides/ckeditor-ai/permissions.html

What’s the difference between WProofreader and CKEditor AI proofreading/grammar checks?
WProofreader acts in a more ‘real-time’ manner — fixing things as you write and displaying mistakes when content is loaded. CKEditor AI checks can be triggered programmatically, suggested changes can be displayed all at once with an option to approve all or approve one by one, etc. Which is better depends on the use case and the amount of content.

⚠️ Internal note: We should be retreating from offering WProofreader because they are becoming a competitor to CKEditor AI — see their AI Writing Assistance functionality: https://wproofreader.com/ai-writing-assistant

Content AI
Add AI suggestions, commands and prompts to your editor. Autocomplete text, stream responses, provide more context, generate images and automatically enrich with tables, marks and more.
Closing + Creating
TipTap’s Rules are designed to work automatically in the background compared to our Review Mode which is on-demand. This enables CKEditor AI to have more control over the cost of operation by running smarter AI queries as well as their final outcome.TipTap offers set of lower-level building blocks for clients to implement their own AI - we are offering a complete product which is well-integrated with the editor and plug-and-play. Tradeoff is that it’s slightly less flexible than TipTap.
Tiptap
AI Agents
The Tiptap AI Agent isn’t just a chatbot. It’s a full-featured document editor powered by AI. It reads, rewrites, plans, and edits rich text using structured tool calls. Just like a real collaborator would.
Closing + Creating
TipTap states their agent understands rich-text, but as far as we know only simplified, markdown formatting. CKEditor AI is the first attempt to make AI work with a HTML content that might be much more complex.Again, less available out-of-the box but more flexibility for devs to implements a custom solution. 
With CKEditor AI, we’re doing a lot of heavy-lifting and we solve hard issues that come with integrating AI with any editor so our customers don’t have to, but we offer less flexibility when it comes to developing a custom solution. This will benefit the user in the long-run because we want to make sure CKEditor AI works flawlessly with 300+ CKEditor features out of the box.

# The 5% Solution for AI Content Creation

## Strategic Guidance for Seeing Real Gains from Artificial Intelligence

[**Chapter One: Where’s the ROI in AI?	2**](#chapter-one:-where’s-the-roi-in-ai?)

[**Chapter Two: The Failure Points in AI Content Creation	3**](#chapter-two:-the-failure-points-in-ai-content-creation)

[**Chapter Three: The C-suite Pressure Mandate	5**](#chapter-three:-the-c-suite-pressure-mandate)

[**Chapter Four: The Hidden Toll of AI Content Tools	6**](#chapter-four:-the-hidden-toll-of-ai-content-tools)

[**Chapter Five: The Key to Using AI Competitively	8**](#chapter-five:-the-key-to-using-ai-competitively)

[**Chapter Six: Turning AI Promises into Potential	11**](#chapter-six:-turning-ai-promises-into-potential)

# 

# Chapter One: Where’s the ROI in AI? {#chapter-one:-where’s-the-roi-in-ai?}

AI has been the largest driving force behind technology advancements over the past few years. Yet, a recent MIT study[^1] found that roughly 95% of generative AI pilots fail to deliver on their value.

Your projects should be in the other 5%. 

The key is to avoid the pitfalls. The MIT study found that “Generic tools like ChatGPT are widely used, but custom solutions stall due to integration complexity and lack of fit with existing workflows.”[^2] 

This hits the nail on the head. AI often gets too complex to integrate and rarely works seamlessly with user workflows. Without these two critical properties, AI won’t make its mark. 

These roadblocks have even affected one of AI’s most promising use cases: content creation and management. Despite being among the first and most obvious applications for generative AI, content-focused pilots still face integration hurdles, poor workflow alignment, and haphazard user adoption. But content creation is ubiquitous for any organization, so solving these challenges can have an outsized impact. 

In this eBook, you’ll discover the main issues with the AI content creation process, why consolidation can solve these points, and the benefits you’ll see from consolidating your content workflows. 

## 

# Chapter Two: The Failure Points in AI Content Creation {#chapter-two:-the-failure-points-in-ai-content-creation}

When generative AI had its breakout moment, people immediately saw its potential for content creation. You could generate outlines or even full drafts, research information, or polish final versions. Businesses expected industry-redefining productivity increases as a result. Yet, businesses soon tempered expectations as the reality failed to meet the promise. Using generative AI for content turned out harder than initially thought. 

Why? Here are a few reasons that AI gains haven’t panned out. 

### Disconnected toolsets and broken workflows

Whether it's customer-facing materials, internal corporate communications, or writing reports, businesses run on content. Everyone across the team can use an assist. 

But while AI should improve productivity, it often fragments workflows, making speed and quality improvements an uphill battle. To produce a finished piece, employees might use: 

* Multiple AI chatbots such as Gemini, ChatGPT, and Claude  
* Grammar checkers like Grammarly or ProWritingAid   
* Content summarization tools like Perplexity or QuillBot  
* Optimization tools like Surfer SEO or Frase

That’s a lot of tab switching (and payment overhead with 4+ tools) simply to create a piece of content. Plus, you get inconsistent results, as no two AI tools answer the same way. Disjointed workflows lead to disjointed results.

### Shadow AI

According to Microsoft, 71% of employees have used unapproved AI tools[^3] on the job. Doing this can introduce problems such as:

* Software vulnerabilities that cyberattackers can exploit  
* Potential compliance breaches, typically by sharing protected information in third-party applications  
* Network strain

If businesses want to maintain strong governance, limiting the use of unauthorized AI tools is absolutely imperative. 

### AI hallucinations

When creating content, you want high-quality, factually accurate output. Yet, pure-play generative AI models can get things wrong. These tools don’t “know” anything; they predict the next word in a sentence. Making these inferences means it can be way off (and lack originality in its responses). 

These hallucinations slow down adoption, lead to errors seeping through, and put speed bumps on productivity gains. You can cut down on hallucinations by giving AI models more context via document uploads or links and by enabling users to check citations for accuracy. Establishing clear AI guardrails, such as policies and systems that ensure responsible, accurate output, also helps keep these models reliable.

### Time-consuming implementation

Integrating AI in your organization takes a lot of time and effort. Development teams have to: 

* Choose the right models or tools.  
* Review benchmarking information.   
* Gather information for more context.  
* Integrate them with your tech stack.  
* Check compatibility with your existing tools.  
* Run QA tests on both software integrations *and* model outputs.

As mentioned in the MIT report, internal builds fail twice as often. Plus, with new models coming out frequently, developers will have to keep up with adjusting their applications to offer the new features and behaviors (and ensure they work with their existing tech stacks). All this development and maintenance work rarely yields the ROI or adoption levels to drive the business forward. 

## Chapter Three: The C-suite Pressure Mandate {#chapter-three:-the-c-suite-pressure-mandate}

C-suite executives were promised deep productivity increases with the advent of generative AI. While these gains haven’t fully materialized, particularly compared to the grandiose promises of full corporate reinvention, efficiency gains and greater output still remain the underlying goal.

Executives remain bullish on AI, with 92% of business leaders claiming they will increase spending on AI initiatives over the next three years.[^4] This puts tremendous pressure on IT teams to deploy AI faster and on business leaders to get their teams to adopt AI effectively. 

But AI can’t just be a problem you throw money at. With businesses starting to abandon their gen AI pilots, the age of experimentation may be coming to a close. The pressure’s just too great. 

If teams want to succeed, they must learn from the early missteps of generative AI pilots. The early days fell victim to a few strategic issues, including: 

* **Selecting the wrong use cases:** Many companies bet on the wrong applications for generative AI. Some aimed too high, building internal models that failed to deliver results. Others applied AI to tasks requiring more precision than a non-deterministic tool could handle. It took time and missteps to identify where AI truly adds value.  
* **Build complexity:** Businesses rushed to create in-house AI systems, only to find them expensive to build, hard to maintain, and quickly outdated as models evolved. The technology was simply too untested for businesses to maintain effectively.  
* **Lack of integration with existing toolsets:** In many organizations, AI adoption became a free-for-all. Users experimented with different tools and brought uneven levels of prompt engineering skills, creating a Wild West situation. Standardizing and integrating AI within existing workflows would have made a far greater impact.

These missteps are understandable as no one wanted to be left in the dust during the AI race. But now, the industry knows more. You have to focus on integrating AI into users’ workflows. Your C-suite expects productivity increases, and that means users must have the tools at the ready. In short, put AI where people work. 

### Content is still king (especially for AI)

Businesses produce a lot of content. Whether it’s one-to-many externally facing marketing materials or thought leadership content, one-to-one email communications from sales or customer support, internal content for intranets or project updates, or even writing reports for higher-ups, content is the beating heart of any organization or workflow. 

In short, content is ripe for efficiency gains. But few users have all their tools in one spot. AI sprawls across multiple services, whether that’s using chatbots like ChatGPT or Gemini, grammar checkers and writing assistants, or research tools. Users have to switch between these tools, which not only nibbles at their productivity, it can drive them to abandon tools or decide to not even try. 

Yet, the pressure from the C-suite continues. Your board is likely already demanding results, questioning when they don’t materialize (or do so unevenly). 

If you want to speed up the content pipeline and unblock your users, not to mention demonstrate measurable improvements to higher-ups, then the key is to consolidate your content tools in one spot. 

To understand why, it’s worth living a day in the life of a content creator. 

# Chapter Four: The Hidden Toll of AI Content Tools {#chapter-four:-the-hidden-toll-of-ai-content-tools}

Content creation isn’t a large monolithic process: It’s a mixture of multiple subprocesses, each with its own interaction points across employees, digital workers like AI, and software solutions.

Let’s take the example of publishing marketing content to a CMS. The table below shows the potential steps and touchpoints:

| Content Stage | Task | Workers involved | Systems involved |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Ideation | Receive request for content or create an idea | Product team or content team | Project management tools |
| Ideation | Research the topic for initial thoughts | Writer | AI chatbot, search, AI summarizer |
| Research and planning | Generate an outline for review | Writer | AI chatbot, SEO AI tools, documents |
| Research and planning | Send outline for review | Writer, subject matter experts | Collaboration documents, project management tools |
| Research and planning | Receive feedback on outline and iterate | Editor, reviewer | Docs, CMS comment thread, AI chatbot |
| Research and planning | Do any further research required | Writer | Search engines, AI assistants |
| Drafting and development | Begin drafting from the outline (can take hours or include multiple iterations | Writer | Word processor, Hemingway, AI assistants |
| Drafting and development | Review content before sending to team | Writer | AI grammar checker, AI style check |
| Review and revision | Send to reviewers for fact-checking, editing, or proofing | Editors, SMEs | CMS workflows, collaborative document tools |
| Review and revision | Receive edits and make minor or major revisions | Writer, editor | CMS, collaboration tools, docs, AI assistants |
| Review and revision | Final proof | Editor / subject matter expert | Grammar checker, AI, research tools, search engines |
| Publishing and delivery | Add to CMS (if not already added) | Content ops | CMS |
| Publishing and delivery | Review in layout | Writer, editor, designer | CMS preview |
| Publishing and delivery | Publish final content | Content manager | CMS |

That’s a lot of back-and-forth between workers, teams, and AI tools. This complexity grows when users switch between multiple tools at each step. This leaves multiple touchpoints where AI can assist, but users must be able to access AI and use it effectively. In a complex process like this, users tend to fall into two buckets. 

The first type may get frustrated and drop AI entirely. Or they may use it haphazardly and inconsistently. Either way, AI starts receding from their daily workflows, leading to spotty adoption at best or top-down, grudging AI adoption mandates at worst (which rarely changes long-term behavior). 

The second group is more nuanced. These users adopt AI and enjoy using it, but they often have to switch between multiple tools to get the job done. This does lead to productivity enhancements, but the results aren’t as strong as they can be. Users may:

* Lose productivity switching between tabs.  
* Believe they’re gaining more time from AI than they actually do.  
* Grow exhausted from cognitive strain, further dampening productivity.  
* Make more mistakes.  
* Rely on multiple models that can contradict each other and introduce errors.

Plus, this group brings their own AI tools, adding to the aforementioned shadow IT risks. They may cause security issues or leak sensitive information that trips you up for compliance. 

The solution for both user groups isn’t more tools; it’s to unify their toolset in their existing workflows. The industry writ large has already kicked off this process, with Google Docs including inline AI rewrites for content using Gemini and AI chatbots integrating search engine results. But these solutions are still piecemeal. Chatbots can’t quickly paste content into your editor (and often, your output depends on your prompt). Google Docs doesn’t really do research. 

If you want to solve the problem, you’ll need something that puts every tool you need in one place. 

# Chapter Five: The Key to Using AI Competitively {#chapter-five:-the-key-to-using-ai-competitively}

The winners in the AI race won’t be those with the most tools: it’ll be those with the rightsized stack. In everyone’s eagerness to remain on the cutting edge of AI, they built frankenstacks of AI tools that turned linear workflows into byzantine processes. This made employees adopt AI at a lower rate and, when they did, they only partially realized AI’s potential.

Instead of bouncing users between tabs, unification turns AI from a burden to a true productivity driver. This chapter explores what consolidation should look like, what strategic benefits it offers, and why simplicity will set your business apart. 

### How it should work

Some software providers have already caught on to this trend. As mentioned, collaborative document editors integrate inline AI edits and chatbots search the web. However, these only complete part of the puzzle: rarely do you see a single source that includes all the content tools needed.

The industry can do even better.

Ideally, users should have a single content editing space that allows them to:

* **Use a full AI chat assistant:** Search, generate outlines, summarize documents, and ask questions with a single interface as you work to speed up every step of the content process.  
* **Edit content inline:** Rewrite sections, get feedback, or tighten elements of the content by highlighting a section and clicking a button. If someone gets stuck with a particularly thorny paragraph, this can unblock them immediately.  
* **Review content:** For most content creators, writing and reviewing are two separate activities. Adding a separate workflow for review matches this approach, allowing users to get feedback across an entire document the way a human reviewer might mark up a collaborative document.

Putting these tools in one spot simplifies the entire content creation workflow and limits the amount of switching. No more flitting between ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, Perplexity, Grammarly, SEO tools, outline creators, and search engines. 

### A new phase in strategic AI deployment

Consolidation isn’t just a usability fix: it’s a strategic differentiator. For years, nearly every business has claimed to have AI as part of their strategic vision—consolidation must be one of the primary objectives to generate ongoing gains. Simplifying the AI stack saves development time, boosts business user productivity, and leads to ongoing ROI. 

Below are how this benefits business users and developers. 

**Benefits for business users**

*Productivity gains that scale*  
Over time, marginal, incremental gains add up. You see this across different domains of life—retirement plan contributions compound, small diet changes lead to weight loss, and manufacturing improvements can cut cycle times. 

So, too with AI. A user might save only ten minutes on a single task, but that can lead to hours across the lifecycle of the workflow for a content piece. Over time, these gains pile up across a user base. If you’re using this on your own organization, this can speed up report production or content production for external use. For anyone developing a commercial-use application, this becomes a major selling point, generating more revenue.

*Greater AI adoption*  
The c-suite wants to see returns. But step one means getting employees to actually *use* AI in their daily workflow. In fact, according to a study from Salesforce, 45% of employees not currently using AI would use it more if it was more fully integrated into their existing technology.[^5] 

Putting AI directly into their editors can spike adoption. It’s the first step toward not only gaining productivity, but building a strong culture of AI literacy and use. 

*Quality and consistency*  
Everyone writes differently. With the breakneck pace of work, inconsistencies creep in. Switching between tools amplifies this problem, turning content creation into a game of telephone between humans and hallucinating AIs. Consolidation provides one AI-assisted environment that aligns tone, enforces brand guidelines, and assists with factual accuracy. 

Many AI discussions miss this point. Productivity isn’t the only benefit: quality and consistency can greatly improve with the right AI tools. When models operate in context, they improve quality, enhancing brand consistency externally and improving user output within the organization.

**Benefits for developers and technical teams**  
The AI rush added significant complexity to tech stacks. Every disconnected tool adds integration work, maintenance overhead, and governance risks. By consolidating tools into a single, composable application component, developers have less to worry about, allowing them to focus on building differentiated features instead of paying down technical debt. Here are a few of the major benefits for development teams. 

*Better security and governance*  
Consolidating your AI systems improves privacy, security, and compliance. It gives you greater control and visibility over systems while shrinking your attack surface and simplifying governance (you’ll only have one system to manage permissions, for instance). Plus, if you’re working with a single provider, you can review their security and compliance certifications to ensure they use the highest levels of security practices, such as SOC 2 Type 2\. 

*Protecting engineering time and speeding up time to market*  
It’s worth reiterating that in-house AI pilots fail at double the rate of integrated components. That’s why it’s crucial to choose a strong provider that has already done the heavy lifting for you. Your team shouldn’t lose time researching and benchmarking models, juggling APIs, and learning the ins and outs of each AI provider. 

Doing so helps you: 

* **Accelerate time to market:** When you can simply add an editor with AI as an application component, your teams deploy embedded AI to users in hours instead of weeks or months. In fact, this likely satisfies the C-suite mandate, as 47% of leaders claim generative AI products are being deployed too slowly.[^6] Also, users can receive the latest AI feature updates without your team having to worry about integrations or upkeep.   
* **Innovate other features:** Offloading the development burden frees up engineering resources to focus on other features and capabilities in your application. Building, integrating, and testing AI is time-consuming. Offloading to a vendor keeps teams focused on innovation where their expertise lies.   
* **Avoid technical debt:** Developing in-house may offer control, but that work is prone to bugs and vulnerabilities. Offloading to a vendor ensures reliable performance without miring your team in constant fixes.

*Be ready for the future*  
AI changes fast, with new models, capabilities, and integration patterns changing almost monthly. When you consolidate your systems and choose to buy from a vendor, you won’t have to rebuild your infrastructure every time. You can let vendors handle this for you, and integrate the best-in-class solutions into your workflows without overburdening your teams. 

# Chapter Six: Turning AI Promises into Potential {#chapter-six:-turning-ai-promises-into-potential}

In many cases, AI has been tall on promises and short on results. People raced to figure out how to integrate generative AI into their organizations only to watch 95% of their pilots falter. The amount of time (and money) thrown at the problem has been astronomical, and people might start to sour on AI. 

Yet, when implemented well, AI can absolutely drive value to your organization. It can boost productivity, user satisfaction, and become a convenient way of producing stronger content. But the key here is to focus on: 

* Strong, focused use cases   
* Adding AI to users’ actual workflows  
* Simplifying integration

That’s what CKEditor AI was built for. 

Remember what the MIT study said. They found that “Generic tools like ChatGPT are widely used, but custom solutions stall due to integration complexity and lack of fit with existing workflows.”[^7] CKEditor keeps you from having to build your own AI solution for content creation and management, yet gives you the flexibility to add AI to nearly any content workflow. 

If you want to give it a shot, start a [14-day free trial today](https://portal.ckeditor.com/signup).

# Chapter one: The fragmented AI workflow {#chapter-one:-the-fragmented-ai-workflow}

Imagine you’re writing a report for a higher-up. You have to make a business case for an important initiative. This requires heavy research and, because decision-makers will see it, the content must be pristine and typo-free. 

While working, you keep multiple tabs open: your main editor, ChatGPT, Gemini, and Claude. You also do research using Google and read reports from analysts or consulting firms. Then, at some point, you need to open yet another tab for Grammarly to ensure there are no typos. Nearing the finish line, you read through again and realize several paragraphs contradict each other (likely from pasting across chatbots). That’s a lot of complexity to complete an assignment (and a lot of overhead to pay for all those tools). 

This is how most people use AI. 

In “[The 5% AI Content Solution](https://ckeditor.com/insights/embedded-ai-for-content-creation-the-5-percent-solution/),” we covered the major barriers to operationalizing artificial intelligence effectively in organizations. Disparate toolsets were the primary roadblock acting as a silent productivity killer. Yet, the C-suite still wants to see returns on their AI investments. 

How do you solve this? Consolidation. Gathering AI tools into one place in employees’ natural workflows reduces errors and boosts productivity over time. As Roman Khudonogov, Head of Product for Tiugo Technologies, put it, “The solution isn’t to ban AI tools or simply accept the patchwork approach. It’s to embed AI capabilities directly into the applications where content creation happens.”[^1]

That’s exactly what CKEditor AI does: It puts AI content creation tools right in the interface of your WYSIWYG editor component that can be added to any application. 

This eBook will show you how to eliminate tool sprawl with CKEditor AI, what this looks like in practice, and why embedding AI into real workflows leads to stronger adoption and better outcomes. 

# Chapter two: Moving toward a more effective AI solution {#chapter-two:-moving-toward-a-more-effective-ai-solution}

The tech industry worked backwards with AI. When ChatGPT burst onto the scene, business leaders saw massive automation and productivity potential. Businesses rushed to find use cases, often adding AI where it wouldn’t naturally fit. The constant refrain of, “If you don’t apply AI now, you’ll be left behind by those who do,” added to the haste. 

Basically, the misstep was that AI became a solution in search of a problem. 

Normally, businesses choose technology to solve a specific problem. If you struggle with organizing sales leads, you buy a CRM. Since it’s a solved product category, it fits within users’ natural workflows. Plus, many employees like the convenience of the new solution since it simplifies their day jobs. Adoption grows, as does morale. 

This AI rush led companies to be reactive, shoehorning it into workflows or pushing adoption without fully considering fit. This dampened adoption and led to visible backlash. For instance:

* Search engines rolled out AI summaries fast, which led to errors and changed how content was created and consumed.  
* SMS and messaging platforms added AI-generated summaries to texts and conversations that people didn’t feel needed summarization.   
* In developer workflows, teams have been pressured to increase AI usage, even when outputs require significant rework.

These bad experiences reduced trust in AI. The data bears this out. Only 5% of Americans say they “trust AI a lot” in a YouGov poll, with the same poll showing that users are particularly skeptical around sensitive areas like healthcare or finance.[^2] Only 41% of people say that they believe and trust what they consume online, which has been driven largely by the growth of AI-driven videos and written content.[^3] The result is skepticism. Add in constant context switching, and you not only have inefficient workflows, but frustrated users who may avoid AI altogether.

So how do you effectively deploy AI? You have to emphasize the end user experience in a way that fits their natural workflows. Consolidating AI tools into one interface fosters a more seamless experience that boosts productivity and increases adoption. 

CKEditor, a rich text editor component integrated with other applications, allows you to handle both objections. AI tools for content creation sit right within the editor as a seamless part of users’ workflows. Nothing is ever forced on your team either: they can choose whether AI is a tool for a specific job or whether they’re better off relying on their own thoughts and writing. 

# Chapter three: AI anywhere people write {#chapter-three:-ai-anywhere-people-write}

CKEditor AI puts an all-in-one AI content suite wherever people write. Want to put it in an LMS? Just add CKEditor to your application. Running an intranet board? Put AI directly into it without much trouble. Selling a health records system to end users? It’s just a plug-in away. 

Anywhere users create content, CKEditor can give them the AI tools to enhance their output. But before diving into CKEditor AI, it helps to have a short overview of CKEditor itself. 

## What is CKEditor? 

CKEditor is a composable rich text editor (RTE) component you can add to any application. It’s comprehensive, with more than 300 features available, and uses a plug-in architecture for easy development and integration. Plus, it’s deeply customizable to fit your use cases. 

The RTE offers multiple solutions to fit nearly any circumstance. You can add a Google docs-like interface for document creation and collaboration, a WYSIWYG HTML editor for web-based content, or tools for creating structured documents like legal briefs or research reports with consistent formatting, citations, and layouts ready for print, to name a few.

In short, CKEditor offers everything you need to enable content creation under any circumstance. 

## CKEditor AI

CKEditor follows two critical design principles: 

* End users should have everything they need to create quality content in one place.  
* Developers should be able to easily integrate the RTE into any application while retaining deep flexibility. 

These two principles—one clean workspace for users, low-friction integration for developers—make AI scalable instead of just another pilot that fizzles. 

Users get every capability they need in one spot. They can use AI tools to brainstorm content, search the web, reason through problems, generate outlines, check grammar, or modify tone without leaving their main editor.  

Developers can easily add AI content creation to any app just by adding the CKEditor component. They set up the component using the pre-made [CKEditor Builder](https://ckeditor.com/ckeditor-5/builder/), selecting which plug-ins to add, and keeping up with releases. CKEditor AI is a plug-in, so it’s easy to add. This increases your product team’s velocity and reduces overall maintenance burden. 

Here’s how it will look in practice. 

### The user experience

From the user’s perspective, CKEditor AI supports the full content lifecycle: research, drafting, editing, refining, and reviewing. It comes down to a few main components: 

* **AI Chat:** This acts as a full sidekick while you write. AI Chat has broad use cases, so this guide will offer a non-exhaustive list to demonstrate how users may boost productivity.   
* **AI Quick actions:** To save time, CKEditor offers several pre-defined actions for repetitive tasks. Developers can also create custom quick actions by defining their own prompts and adding them to the menu.   
* **AI Review:** AI Review lets you receive feedback and edits, including grammar and tone suggestions, once you’ve completed writing. No need to jump to a separate grammar editor. Additionally, developers and integrators can add custom review instructions.   
* **AI Translate:** With teams and customers being globally distributed, having access to quick translations can help speed up content creation. The AI Translate feature lets you convert text across languages without having to leave the editor. 

#### AI Chat

CKEditor AI’s Chat appears directly within the editor and can reference and modify the document you’re currently working on, reducing the convoluted copy/paste workflow. Working on the exact document has another benefit: it can preserve formatting. LLMs struggle with rich text and HTML. CKEditor AI works directly on the editor’s structured HTML, allowing it to retain headings, lists, links, and other formatting without manual cleanup. 

You can enter any prompt you want here, similar to another chatbot. That does leave it open-ended, however. So here are a few examples. 

![][image1]

*Figure 1: Document and AI Chat interface*

**Content selection, editing, or manipulation**

Ask CKEditor AI to edit your document so far. Let’s say you wanted to improve readability. You could enter a prompt asking it to: 

* Highlight complex sentences.  
* Look for passive voice and suggest active voice alternatives.  
* Ask for layout suggestions to improve legibility.

This example asks the chat for readability design improvements. 

![][image2]

*Figure 2: Prompting the AI for readability formatting improvements*

In this same example, you could ask it to automatically apply these changes. You can’t do that with a separate chatbot. This just offers massive time savings and content quality improvements. Small elements like these can really add up over time. 

![][image3]

*Figure 3: Suggestions added in the chat. Users can apply, edit, or reject a selection.* 

*![][image4]*

*Figure 4: All suggestion applied to the document with one click.* 

**Brainstorming and generating outlines**

Whether you’re creating an internal FAQ, writing customer interaction notes, or drafting a blog post, CKEditor AI can brainstorm angles or generate outlines. For instance, you could add a prompt such as: 

“Brainstorm a new article for me. I want to write a report detailing the most recent changes in artificial intelligence as it pertains to regulated industries. This report will be given internally to tech team leaders, so please generate an outline that analyzes strengths and weaknesses.”

From there, you’ll receive a response in the chat along the lines of:

*Figure 7: Outline is added to the main document with one click of the “Apply” button.*

This can get you started on the report, and you can even have it populate the blank document with your outline, so you just have to fill in the sections.

**Adding context or summarizing content**

For research-heavy tasks or regulated environments, drafting often depends on external sources. You can get more tailored responses by adding context from URLs or uploading files.

Additionally, you can have the AI directly summarize the document, which is helpful for lengthy reports: 

![][image8] 

*Figure 8: Asking for a document summary using AI chat.* 

*![][image9]*

*Figure 9: The chat returns a summary.* 

#### AI Quick Actions

AI Chat is only one way to interact with CKEditor AI. If you have an open-ended question, chat’s the way to go. But some tasks recur enough that re-prompting gets repetitive. That’s why CKEditor offers AI Quick Actions. 

Within a document, you can highlight a selection of text and use AI to:

* Ask AI questions directly   
* Get writing improvements and suggestions  
* Have it complete sentences or paragraphs  
* Make text shorter or longer  
* Change the tone output to pre-defined styles, such as casual or professional  
* Fix grammar

Additionally, developers can create additional custom quick actions using their own prompts. This lets you save even more time for end users. 

![][image10]

*Figure 10: A list of AI Quick Actions on a selected highlight.* 

![][image11]

*Figure 11: Change to the section after choosing to adjust the length and asking AI to make the selection shorter.* 

#### AI Review 

After finishing your draft, you’ll want to check it for quality. CKEditor’s AI Review feature helps do this for you. 

AI Review evaluates the entire document for clarity, readability, and tone (or anything else you want, as you can modify with your own prompts). It suggests changes in the document itself, which you can then accept or reject. This leads to strong, high-quality text without having to take up extra cycles from a review or editorial team.

![][image12]

*Figure 12: Clicking review in the toolbar offers multiple options for review type.* 

*![][image13]*

*Figure 13: Suggestions are added. Users can accept or dismiss individual edits or apply all changes.* 

#### AI Translate 

When creating content for global audiences, translation can quickly become a bottleneck. CKEditor’s AI Translate feature lets you convert content into other languages directly inside the editor. It currently supports a number of languages out of the box, including English, Spanish, French, German, Chinese (Simplified), Japanese, Russian, Portuguese, Korean, and Italian. However, you aren’t limited: your team can add support for additional custom languages to meet your market or team needs. AI Translate’s UI also makes it easy to review and manage AI-generated suggestions.

![][image14]

*Figure 14: Clicking Translate in the toolbar offers multiple language options to choose from.*

![][image15]

*Figure 15: The AI has translated the document into French as a preview. The user can click “Accept Translation” or “Cancel.”*

### The developer experience

CKEditor was built for developer friendliness. It’s easy to integrate, scalable, and minimizes long-term maintenance. This extends to CKEditor AI as well. Instead of bolting on another AI service, it integrates easily into a well-developed and maintained architecture. 

#### Reduced engineering lift

Integrating AI tools into an existing rich text editor or an application sounds easy at first. But you have to:

* Research and benchmark AI models for different tasks.  
* License one or more LLM models.  
* Develop integrations for your applications.  
* Bring together several models and tools that fit across multiple workflows  
* Route users to the correct model if offering multiple options.  
* Test and ensure all AI suggestions operate well without breaking critical elements of your rich text editor.  
* Build an interface for presenting AI suggestions in the application.   
* Let users reject, accept, or edit suggestions.   
* Ensure AI content preserves formatting in the editor.   
* Maintain and update as models evolve.

That’s a lot of work for teams already stretched thin. Plus, because systems are interconnected, one weak link can derail the project, cause instability, or lead to regressions. 

CKEditor AI simplifies this. Instead of doing all of this yourself, just add AI as a plug-in. CKEditor AI manages model orchestration, moderation, telemetry, and streaming responses as part of the platform, removing the need for teams to build and maintain these systems themselves. 

Also, development teams won’t have to worry about creating content formatting rules. CKEditor provides a full AI editing layer that works directly with the editor’s structured HTML and formatting model so the AI can work on the document itself without breaking formatting. 

Additionally, CKEditor provides a strong UI for reviewing and accepting changes. CKEditor has already built a seamless interface to support cross-team collaboration, including support for suggested changes and comments. This same interface works smoothly for AI suggestions, saving you the time and hassle of building and testing a complex UI yourself. 

For cloud deployments, the service and model updates are handled automatically. With on-prem deployments, teams simply update the CKEditor AI container within their environment.

Once CKEditor AI is integrated, developers can further customize and extend AI capabilities to match their application’s needs.

#### Developer flexibility and customization

CKEditor integrates into modern tech stacks. Its plug-in based architecture lets you pick and choose which features to add to any editor (including AI). This gives developers:

* **Fine-grained AI control:** Tailor contextual prompts, customize the UI, and set boundaries for when and how users access AI.   
* **Flexible deployment:** CKEditor AI is available using both on-premises and via SaaS to ensure it can meet your requirements.   
* **Multiple provider options:** Choose which models to make available for your users from multiple LLM providers and models. Select from OpenAI, Google, or Anthropic.   
* **Domain-specific customization:** Extend CKEditor AI to match your industry’s terminology, compliance needs, or customer use cases.  
* **Prebuilt frontend components:** Ship prebuilt, configurable UI components such as dropdowns, modals, and reviewing interfaces.   
* **Support for the MCP protocol:** Get full Model Context Protocol (MCP) support on-premises. You can add custom tools such as searching databases, integrating third-party services, or even integrating your own custom AI models.   
* **Server-side extensibility:** Add custom server endpoints and prompt templates for your own workflows or to integrate additional services. 

The result is a developer experience that reduces integration risk, limits long-term maintenance overhead, and accelerates time to value.

#### Security

Security can be a barrier against enterprise AI adoption. Because CKEditor AI sits within the wider framework of the editor ecosystem itself, it inherits its strong security foundation and protections. 

CKEditor safeguards your data with:

* Data encryption at rest with AES-256 and in transit with TLS 1.2+.  
* Proactive security measures including a secure software development lifecycle, regular security audits, and patches to address vulnerabilities.  
* Continuous monitoring and incident response.  
* Regular penetration tests.  
* Compliance with rigorous security standards, including SOC 2 Type 2\.

Additionally, CKEditor AI specifically includes: 

* An on-premises deployment option for additional control. If you have specific data compliance requirements, please [contact our team](https://ckeditor.com/contact-sales/) to discuss your deployment needs.   
* Content moderation that filters out inappropriate content.   
* Granular permissions to ensure only the right users can access specific features and models.   
* Encryption for all AI conversations, documents, and uploaded files.   
* Resilience and reliability with automatic model switching if one model fails, stream error recovery, and automatic retries on temporary errors. 

#### AI governance

CKEditor AI isn’t just easy to integrate: it also provides the infrastructure needed to run AI reliably inside applications at scale. Key capabilities include:

* **Telemetry:** Gain insights into AI usage and performance without adding extra instrumentation.  
* **Error handling:** Automatically catch and manage failures, keeping your editor stable.  
* **Content moderation:** Ensure outputs meet compliance and safety standards out of the box.  
* **Streaming output:** Deliver AI suggestions in real time for a smooth user experience.

By handling these complex systems for you, CKEditor AI is enterprise-ready from day one, letting your team focus on building value rather than infrastructure.

# Chapter four: Who owns the AI infrastructure? {#chapter-four:-who-owns-the-ai-infrastructure?}

At this point, you’ve seen how CKEditor AI works. The remaining question isn’t whether embedded AI is valuable, it’s where AI lives in your stack and who must maintain it over time. 

**The cost of owning AI infrastructure**

Even without AI, rich text editors are full of edge cases across browsers, collaboration states, and security. Adding AI multiplies that complexity.

Adding AI separately to an editor or application introduces model selection, testing, prompt design, and ongoing integration work. Even if you have an editor in place, layering on AI features instead of selecting them as part of core infrastructure just creates even more complexity that requires ongoing attention. That’s a lot of work for teams already stretched thin. 

**Volatility and maintenance**

AI technology changes fast. Too fast. Models and protocols evolve and aren’t always reliable upon release before a few tweaks. APIs update as do the features available. Pricing changes from time to time. Security issues will arise (as they do with any technology). If you integrate only one model, which is an undertaking of its own, you may need to change downstream, which requires your team to own regressions, testing, and migrations. 

Plus, you have to consider the user experience: 

* How will you fit AI into the interface that’s seamless enough to drive adoption?   
* Can you standardize some prompts for end users to drive consistent quality output? This will also make using AI far more convenient.   
* How will you add context to the output such as business documents or style guides? This also helps standardize quality.   
* Does the AI you integrate help or hinder accessibility?   
* Do AI suggestions preserve formatting on documents in your editor? Can it work with your editor’s features without breaking anything?

On top of this, over time, teams must keep code performant, stable, and secure. What starts as just a feature addition becomes the care and feeding of a new ecosystem of capabilities. 

**Opportunity costs**

Unifying these themes, you’ll see that your teams will spend a lot of time maintaining infrastructure rather than working on your core product. Every hour spent managing model changes, re-testing prompts, debugging code, or responding to new security requirements is an hour they’re not engaged in other mission-critical tasks. AI becomes part of your roadmap whether you planned for it or not.

Offloading this to CKEditor shifts the infrastructure burden away from your team. This frees your team up for other value-adds. For commercial applications, that means not missing features that could land new customers or expand reach. For internal applications, it means focusing on improvements that make employees more effective.

# Chapter five: What embedded AI changes for your business {#chapter-five:-what-embedded-ai-changes-for-your-business}

What does this mean for your business? When AI is embedded directly into existing workflows, the benefits compound. CKEditor AI shifts the infrastructure burden away from your team and creates the conditions for measurable, scalable gains.

**Productivity gains that actually scale**

Instead of a convoluted workflow, users have all the capabilities they need in one place. Each time they use AI Chat or a Quick Action, they avoid switching tabs. That reduces errors and saves time. Users don’t just generate faster drafts; they improve the quality of their output, reducing rework. Over time, these incremental gains compound into meaningful productivity improvements.

If you’re building a commercial application, shorter cycle times between ideation and production allow teams to experiment more and ship faster. That velocity becomes a competitive advantage.

**Greater consistency** 

Brand trust comes from consistency. Mistakes or inconsistencies erode credibility, whether with customers or internal stakeholders. Disparate workflows increase these risks, especially when tab switching, and multiple models introduce subtle differences in tone or logic.

The dedicated AI Review helps standardize outputs and improve quality. It also reduces back-and-forth in the review cycle, allowing content to move through approval processes faster.

**Better security**

Shadow IT has long challenged InfoSec teams. AI adds to this, exacerbating privacy concerns. When employees rely on multiple external tools, organizations lose visibility into where data is sent and how it’s processed. 

Consolidating AI capabilities into a single, governed system reduces tool sprawl and unmonitored data exposure. Centralized governance improves monitoring, auditing, and overall control.

**Greater adoption**

Most generative AI pilots fail. The cause isn’t the tech: it’s that it never becomes part of real workflows. When AI lives in separate tools, adoption stalls and measurable impact never shows up.

Embedding AI directly into the systems people already use changes that. Instead of launching another isolated pilot, you activate AI inside existing processes. That drives faster adoption, reduces execution risk, and shortens the path from deployment to real results.

**Improved employee satisfaction**

Employee feelings about AI are mixed. Users adopt AI, but it’s unevenly distributed. Some dive in headfirst, using multiple separate tools while others foreswear AI use altogether. 

When AI fits naturally within existing workflows, resistance drops. Users don’t feel forced to change how they work or manage yet another tool. Instead, they see clear productivity gains with less context switching, fewer errors, and less rework.

# Summary: Scaling AI without the complexity {#summary:-scaling-ai-without-the-complexity}

Content creation is core to any business, whether that’s external marketing communications, internal reports, or one-to-one customer communications from sales or support. Artificial intelligence can improve this experience, leading to greater efficiency and effectiveness. 

Yet, too often, AI gains fail to materialize. This isn’t a flaw in the technology; it’s a mismatch in how it’s deployed. 

Employees must juggle multiple tools, manage inconsistent outputs, and figure out how best to use AI. When this happens, productivity stalls and skepticism grows. Instead, consolidating tools and offering them to users in their existing workflows solves these problems. Embedding AI into the systems employees already use reduces tool sprawl, lowers maintenance burden, and helps AI drive measurable value. 

CKEditor AI is built around that principle. It brings AI into the editor, gives developers control, and allows organizations to scale AI without adding complexity. In short, it brings AI where people already write. 

If you want to put CKEditor AI to use in your organization, please contact our team. They will be happy to help you learn more about CKEditor AI and how it might apply to your organization. 

[Contact the CKEditor Team](https://ckeditor.com/contact-sales/)
